description: Complete osquery command and workflow reference from basic to advanced endpoint monitoring and detection

commands.explanation: Launches the interactive osquery shell for live ad-hoc system querying.
Displays the list of available command-line flags and options for the interactive shell.
Lists the available meta-commands within the interactive shell environment.
Lists all available tables that can be queried on the current operating system.
Displays the structure and column definitions for a specific table.
Sets the output display mode to pretty-print, line, list, or csv for better readability.
Exits the interactive osquery shell safely.
Retrieves the currently installed version of the osquery agent.

commands.example: SELECT hostname, cpu_brand, physical_memory FROM system_info;
commands.explanation: Queries core hardware and operating system details including hostname and memory.
Checks how long the system has been running since the last reboot.

commands.example: SELECT name, major, minor, patch FROM os_version;
commands.explanation: Details the specific operating system version and build number.

commands.example: SELECT uid, username, description FROM users;
commands.explanation: Lists all local user accounts present on the system.
Lists all local groups defined on the system.

commands.example: SELECT user, tty, host, time FROM logged_in_users;
commands.explanation: Identifies users currently logged into the system and their source.

commands.example: SELECT username, time, type FROM last;
commands.explanation: Reviews the history of system logins and reboots.

commands.example: SELECT pid, name, path, cmdline FROM processes;
commands.explanation: Enumerates all currently running processes on the host.

commands.example: SELECT pid, key, value FROM process_envs WHERE pid = 1;
commands.explanation: inspects the environment variables associated with a specific process ID.

commands.example: SELECT pid, fd, path FROM process_open_files WHERE pid = <PID>;
commands.explanation: Lists all file descriptors currently opened by a specific process.

commands.example: SELECT pid, path, permissions FROM process_memory_map WHERE pid = <PID>;
commands.explanation: Examines the memory map of a process to see loaded shared libraries.

commands.example: SELECT port, protocol, address FROM listening_ports;
commands.explanation: Identifies ports that are currently in a listening state on the host.

commands.example: SELECT pid, local_port, remote_address, remote_port FROM process_open_sockets;
commands.explanation: Maps active network sockets to the processes that own them.

commands.example: SELECT interface, address, mask FROM interface_addresses;
commands.explanation: Lists the IP addresses assigned to network interfaces.
Checks the DNS servers configured for the system.
Reads the static host definitions from the hosts file.

commands.example: SELECT path, size, mode, uid FROM file WHERE path = '/etc/passwd';
commands.explanation: Retrieves metadata for a specific file on the disk.

commands.example: SELECT path, sha256 FROM hash WHERE path = '/bin/ls';
commands.explanation: Calculates the cryptographic hash (MD5, SHA1, SHA256) of a specific file.

commands.example: SELECT path, filename FROM file WHERE directory = '/etc/' AND filename LIKE '%.conf';
commands.explanation: Performs a wildcard search for files within a specific directory.

commands.example: SELECT name, version, status FROM deb_packages;
commands.explanation: Lists installed Debian-based packages (Ubuntu/Debian systems).

commands.example: SELECT name, version, release FROM rpm_packages;
commands.explanation: Lists installed RPM-based packages (RHEL/CentOS systems).
Enumerates globally installed Python libraries.

commands.example: SELECT name, path, status FROM startup_items;
commands.explanation: Checks for items configured to run automatically at system boot.
Audits scheduled cron jobs for all users.

commands.example: SELECT id, active_state, load_state FROM systemd_units;
commands.explanation: Inspects systemd services and their current status on Linux systems.
Lists loaded kernel modules or drivers which could indicate rootkits.
Starts the osquery daemon using a specific flag file for configuration.
Validates the syntax of the configuration file without starting the daemon.
Enables verbose logging to standard output for debugging daemon issues.
Specifies the location to write the daemon's process ID file.
Executes a query and outputs the result in strict JSON format.
Executes a query and outputs the result in CSV format.
Configures the daemon to write logs to the local filesystem.
Sets the specific directory where filesystem logs will be stored.
Sets the strictness of the watchdog process that monitors daemon resource usage.
Limits the memory (in MB) the daemon can use before being restarted.
Limits the CPU utilization percentage the daemon is allowed to consume.

commands.example: SELECT pid, path, time FROM process_events;
commands.explanation: Queries the real-time event buffer for process executions (requires audit enabling).

commands.example: SELECT target_path, action, time FROM file_events;
commands.explanation: Queries the event buffer for file system changes monitored by FIM configurations.

commands.example: SELECT local_port, remote_address, action FROM socket_events;
commands.explanation: Queries the event buffer for network socket connections and binds.
Ensures the eventing subsystem is enabled for table based auditing.
Sets the time in seconds before events expire from the internal buffer.
Defines the remote TLS server hostname for configuration and log shipping.
Points to the file containing the shared secret used for authenticating to the TLS server.
Instructs the daemon to pull its configuration from the remote TLS server.

commands.example: SELECT p.pid, p.name, u.username FROM processes p JOIN users u ON p.uid = u.uid;
commands.explanation: Correlates running processes with the specific user account running them.

commands.example: SELECT p.name, l.port, l.protocol FROM listening_ports l JOIN processes p ON l.pid = p.pid;
commands.explanation: Identifies exactly which process binary is responsible for an open port.
Audits the current iptables ruleset to ensure firewall policy compliance.

commands.example: SELECT device, path, type FROM mounts;
commands.explanation: Verifies that filesystems are mounted with correct options (e.g., noexec on tmp).
Lists all public keys authorized to SSH into user accounts.

commands.example: SELECT path, mode FROM file WHERE directory = '/etc/' AND mode LIKE '%777';
commands.explanation: Scans critical directories for files that are writable by any user.

commands.example: SELECT * FROM processes WHERE cmdline LIKE '%/dev/tcp/%';
commands.explanation: Hunts for common bash reverse shell patterns in command lines.

commands.example: SELECT p1.name AS parent, p2.name AS child FROM processes p1 JOIN processes p2 ON p1.pid = p2.parent WHERE p1.name = 'httpd' AND p2.name = 'bash';
commands.explanation: Identifies web server processes spawning unexpected shells.

commands.example: SELECT name, pid, on_disk FROM processes WHERE on_disk = 0;
commands.explanation: Finds processes running from binaries that have been deleted from the disk.

commands.example: SELECT * FROM process_open_sockets WHERE remote_port > 1024 AND state = 'ESTABLISHED';
commands.explanation: Monitors for connections to non-standard high ports often used for C2.

commands.example: SELECT pid, cmdline FROM processes WHERE name = 'nc' OR name = 'netcat';
commands.explanation: Scans active processes for the presence of the netcat utility.

commands.example: SELECT * FROM shell_history WHERE command LIKE '%base64%';
commands.explanation: Searches user shell history for potentially obfuscated base64 commands.

commands.example: SELECT p.name, p.pid, h.sha256 FROM processes p JOIN hash h ON p.path = h.path;
commands.explanation: Calculates the SHA256 hash of the binary for every running process.
Exports the full process state to JSON for offline analysis during an incident.
Captures a snapshot of all files held open by processes for forensic review.

commands.example: SELECT path, size FROM file WHERE directory = '/tmp' AND size > 0;
commands.explanation: Quickly inspects temporary directories for dropped executable payloads.
Verifies the metadata of the sudoers file to check for unauthorized modification.
Feeds a file containing multiple SQL queries directly into the osquery shell.
Introspects the currently loaded query packs defined in the configuration.
Displays the execution schedule and performance stats of configured queries.
Loads an external extension to add new tables or capabilities to osquery.
Connects the shell to a daemon running on a non-standard socket path.
Retrieves high-level information about the Docker daemon if present.

commands.example: SELECT id, image, status FROM docker_containers;
commands.explanation: Lists active Docker containers and their current running status.

commands.example: SELECT label, path, run_at_load FROM launchd;
commands.explanation: Audits macOS launch daemons and agents for persistence.
Checks if macOS Gatekeeper security assessment is enabled.

commands.example: SELECT name, display_name, status FROM services;
commands.explanation: Lists installed services and their running state on Windows.

commands.example: SELECT name, data FROM registry WHERE key LIKE '%Run%';
commands.explanation: Queries the Windows registry for keys that execute programs at startup.
Forces the daemon to flush logs to the configured backend immediately.
Terminates the osquery daemon process (Linux/Unix).
Manually removes the pidfile if the daemon crashed or did not exit cleanly.
Verifies from the system shell that the osquery daemon is running.
